text: The surgeon prepares a glove port using a sterile size seven and a half glove, cutting the digits to fit a ten millimeter trocar for the camera and two five millimeter working ports. A vertical inframullical incision is made using the open Hassan technique, extending the fascial opening to two centimeters to fit the Alexis wound retractor. The glove is attached to the retractor, which is inverted to ensure a tight seal.

The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum and begins laparoscopy by inserting instruments and the camera through the facial defect. The laparoscopic view reveals significant disease in the right sub-diaphragmatic space and around the cecum, along with numerous small deposits on the small bowel and its mesentery, which were not visible on preoperative imaging.

The surgeon closes the fascial defect using standard suturing techniques, followed by closing the skin with a subcuticular suture. The port site remains intact for potential excision during future cytoreductive surgery.

During the single-port laparoscopy, the surgeon observes diffuse peritoneal abnormality in the upper abdomen, while the lower abdomen appears relatively unaffected. The appendix is not identified as the source of the malignancy, and the small bowel appears normal.
A cold biopsy is performed on the abnormal peritoneum in the upper abdomen, with the tissue sample being collected for further histopathological and immunohistochemical analysis.

The surgeon performs a complete staging laparoscopy on a sixty-year-old female with presumed pseudomyxoma peritonei. The glove port is established with the patient in the supine position, followed by an initial sweep of the abdomen to assess the peritoneal surfaces.

The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg to visualize the peritoneum of the right hemidiaphragm, falciform ligament, and right liver lobe. The liver is gently deflected anteriorly to inspect its undersurface and the gallbladder. The left liver lobe is deflected to view the lesser omentum, stomach's lesser curvature, and porta hepatis, while inspecting the left hemidiaphragm. With the table's left side elevated, the spleen is visualized with gentle traction on the omentum. The greater omentum is elevated to inspect it and expose the transverse colon and mesocolon. The proximal omentum is elevated to visualize the proximal transverse colon, showing it tethered to a tumor mass in the right iliac fossa. The patient is then placed in Trendelenburg to visualize the pelvic peritoneum and viscera, scanning the left lower parietal peritoneum, sigmoid colon, and small bowel loops. A small bowel walk is performed to examine the serosal surface and mesentery, inspecting the entire small bowel.
